_meta.authoringNotes: Classification and management per Coughlin & Mann 9th ed. (2014). Surgical outcomes from Coughlin et al. FAI 2002 (PMID 11848061), Boffeli et al. JFAS 2014 (PMID 24210501). Conservative treatment efficacy per Schrier et al. JFAR 2016 (PMID 26851480). Epidemiology per US National Foot Health Assessment 2014 (APMA).

h1Title: Hammer Toe: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Hammer toe is a fixed flexion deformity of the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joint of one or more lesser toes, leaving the toe bent downward in a hammer-like shape. The American Podiatric Medical Association's National Foot Health Assessment estimates that around 7-9% of US adults have at least one hammer toe, rising to nearly 30% in adults over 60. Tight, narrow, or high-heeled footwear and inherited foot shapes are the leading causes; over time the flexible deformity becomes rigid as the joint capsule shortens. The bent toe rubs against the top of the shoe, producing painful corns over the PIP joint and calluses on the metatarsal head, ulcerating in patients with diabetes or peripheral vascular disease. Conservative care — wider footwear, toe pads, splints, and stretching — controls symptoms in 60-70% of flexible deformities; rigid deformities and refractory cases respond to surgical correction with success rates above 80%.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 7-9% of US adults; up to 30% in adults over 60 (APMA National Foot Health Assessment 2014)
keyStats.demographics: Women affected 4-5x more often than men, attributed to high-heeled and narrow footwear; risk rises with age
keyStats.avgAge: Most patients present in their 50s-70s; younger onset in those with neuromuscular disease or familial pes cavus
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated tens of millions globally; foot deformity is among the leading causes of foot pain in adults

definition: Hammer toe (ICD-10: M20.4) is an acquired flexion deformity of the proximal interphalangeal joint of one or more lesser toes (2nd through 5th), often accompanied by hyperextension of the metatarsophalangeal joint and either hyperextension or neutral position of the distal interphalangeal joint. The deformity develops from an imbalance between the long flexor and extensor tendons and the intrinsic foot muscles. The extensors fire at the metatarsophalangeal joint without effective intrinsic muscle support, and the long flexors overpull at the proximal interphalangeal joint, leaving the toe permanently bent. Hammer toe is classified as flexible (passively correctable), semirigid, or rigid (fixed), and surgical planning depends on this assessment. The second toe is most commonly affected because of its length and proximity to a bunion. The condition is managed by podiatrists, foot and ankle orthopedic surgeons, and primary care physicians. It overlaps with two related deformities: claw toe (flexion at both PIP and DIP joints, plus MTP hyperextension) and mallet toe (flexion of the DIP joint alone). Hammer toe is benign in young, well-vascularized patients but a marker of high ulceration risk in diabetes and peripheral arterial disease.

typesClassification.description: The proximal interphalangeal joint can be passively straightened. Conservative treatment with footwear modification, padding, and splinting effectively controls symptoms in most patients.

Partial passive correction is possible but the deformity returns immediately on release. May still respond to bracing and stretching for symptom control; surgery considered if conservative care fails.
The joint cannot be passively straightened because of capsular and tendinous contracture. Conservative care offers symptomatic relief only; surgical correction is the definitive treatment.

typesClassification.type: Hammer toe with metatarsalgia or plantar plate dysfunction
typesClassification.description: Hammer toe coexisting with pain under the corresponding metatarsal head. The plantar plate of the MTP joint may be torn or attenuated, allowing dorsal subluxation. Combined correction addresses both joints.

typesClassification.type: Hammer toe associated with bunion (hallux valgus)
typesClassification.description: The deviated great toe pushes the second toe upward and laterally, accelerating second-toe hammer toe formation. Combined correction is typically necessary.

primarySymptoms: Visible upward bending of the proximal interphalangeal joint of a lesser toe with the toe tip pointing downward.
Painful corn (hyperkeratosis) developing on the dorsal aspect of the proximal interphalangeal joint where the toe rubs against the top of the shoe.
Callus on the ball of the foot under the corresponding metatarsal head from altered weight distribution.
Pain on walking, especially in closed-toe shoes, that improves when barefoot or in open sandals.
Stiffness in the affected toe that worsens through the day and persists in established rigid deformities.
Difficulty fitting shoes; previously comfortable footwear now feels tight over the toes.
Redness, swelling, or skin breakdown over the bent joint, especially in tight footwear.
Pain or numbness in the toe tip from chronic pressure on the dorsal interphalangeal joint.
Worsening of an adjacent bunion as the deformities reinforce each other.
Ulceration over the bent joint or under the metatarsal head in patients with diabetes or peripheral vascular disease.
earlyWarningSigns: Subtle upward bending at the proximal interphalangeal joint of the second or third toe that begins to catch on the inside top of shoes
A small corn forming on the top of one toe joint
New callus under the ball of the foot in the same row as the affected toe
Persistent rubbing or shoe pressure that requires repeated padding to manage
Progressive cosmetic deformity of one or more toes over months to years
emergencySigns: Open ulcer over the bent joint or under the metatarsal head — high risk of infection, especially in diabetes
Spreading redness, warmth, drainage, or fever — cellulitis or osteomyelitis must be excluded
Sudden severe pain, color change to white or blue, or loss of pulses — exclude critical limb ischemia
Black or gangrenous tissue at the toe tip or pressure area — admit for vascular and surgical assessment
Severe pain after recent surgery with swelling, warmth, or drainage from the operative site — exclude wound infection or compartment issue

causes.cause: Footwear with a narrow toe box or high heels
causes.description: Shoes that compress the toes laterally and force them downward over years produce the muscle-tendon imbalance that drives hammer toe formation. Women's fashion footwear is the leading cause of acquired hammer toe in the developed world; men with pointed boots are also at risk.

Long second toes (Morton's foot), pes cavus (high arch), and ligamentous laxity predispose to hammer toe by altering the force distribution across the MTP and PIP joints. A family history of hammer toes is reported in roughly 25-30% of patients.

Bunion deformity pushes the great toe laterally against the second toe, forcing it upward and accelerating hammer toe formation. Roughly half of patients with significant bunions develop second-toe hammer toe over time.

Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease, post-stroke spasticity, diabetic motor neuropathy, multiple sclerosis, and other conditions affecting intrinsic foot muscles produce extensor-flexor imbalance that leads to hammer or claw toes. Onset is often younger and progression more rapid.

A poorly healed proximal phalanx fracture, MTP dislocation, or capsular tear can leave the toe bent. Plantar plate injury under the second MTP joint is a recognized post-traumatic cause of progressive hammer-toe deformity.

Rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, and gout damage MTP and PIP joints and reshape forefoot mechanics, producing combined hammer toe, claw toe, and bunion deformities. The 'rheumatoid forefoot' is a classic presentation requiring specialist care.

riskFactors.factor: Long-term wear of tight, narrow, or high-heeled shoes
riskFactors.description: Repeated compression of the toes over years produces the muscle-tendon imbalance that creates hammer toe. Switching to wider shoes early in the deformity halts progression in many patients.
Women are 4-5x more affected than men, primarily because of footwear choices but also because of slightly greater ligamentous laxity. Postmenopausal weight gain compounds forefoot pressure.

Prevalence rises from under 3% in adults under 40 to roughly 30% in those over 60 (APMA). Connective tissue laxity, fat-pad atrophy under the metatarsals, and decades of cumulative footwear pressure all contribute.
Bunions force the great toe laterally and crowd the second toe upward, accelerating hammer toe formation. Treating both deformities together yields better outcomes than addressing them in isolation.
A second toe longer than the great toe encounters earlier shoe-tip pressure and disproportionate floor load, predisposing to PIP flexion deformity.
High-arched feet have weakened intrinsic muscles and increased dorsiflexion forces at the MTP joints, producing extensor-flexor imbalance and combined hammer/claw deformities.

Motor neuropathy weakens intrinsic foot muscles and shifts forefoot pressure; combined with sensory loss and microvascular disease, this elevates ulceration and amputation risk substantially in deformed toes.

affectedDemographics: Estimated 7-9% of US adults overall and up to 30% in adults over 60 (APMA National Foot Health Assessment).
Women aged 50-75 are the largest patient group seen in podiatry clinics.
Adults with longstanding diabetes mellitus carry roughly 2-3x higher prevalence and substantially higher ulceration risk.
Patients with rheumatoid arthritis develop forefoot deformities including hammer toes in 50-70% during disease course.
Neuromuscular disease populations (Charcot-Marie-Tooth, post-stroke, MS) show earlier and more rapid progression.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is clinical. The history covers pain, footwear difficulties, occupation and lifestyle demands, comorbidities (diabetes, vascular disease, inflammatory arthritis, neuromuscular conditions), and any history of trauma. Examination begins with the patient standing and walking to assess overall foot posture (pes cavus, planus, hallux valgus, hindfoot alignment). The affected toes are inspected for the characteristic dorsiflexed MTP and flexed PIP appearance, dorsal corns, plantar metatarsal callus, and skin breakdown. The deformity is tested for reducibility by passively pushing up on the metatarsal head and seeing whether the toe straightens — full correction indicates a flexible deformity, partial correction a semirigid one, and no correction a rigid one. The plantar plate is assessed with the dorsal drawer test of the second MTP joint to detect attenuation or rupture. Neurovascular examination documents pedal pulses, capillary refill, monofilament sensation, and tendon reflexes. Weight-bearing radiographs (anteroposterior, lateral, oblique views) confirm the deformity in three planes, identify associated bunion, metatarsus primus varus, MTP subluxation, and any degenerative changes. Vascular assessment with ankle-brachial index is essential before surgery in any patient with diabetes, smoking history, or claudication.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the hammer toe deformity and identifies coexisting forefoot and hindfoot abnormalities
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient stands and walks barefoot in the clinic. The clinician notes alignment of the great toe, lesser toes, arch height, and any compensatory gait pattern. 5-10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Classifies the deformity as flexible, semirigid, or rigid, guiding conservative versus surgical management
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician pushes upward under the metatarsal head while the patient relaxes the foot. Full straightening indicates flexible; partial indicates semirigid; no change indicates rigid. Quick painless test.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents joint position, MTP subluxation, bunion, and degenerative change; essential for surgical planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standing radiographs of both feet take 5-10 minutes. Films are read by the podiatric or orthopedic surgeon. Low radiation dose.

diagnosticTests.test: Dorsal drawer test of the second MTP joint
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects plantar plate attenuation or rupture, which often coexists with second-toe hammer toe
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician stabilizes the metatarsal and pushes the proximal phalanx dorsally. Translation greater than 50% of the joint surface or pain suggests plantar plate dysfunction. Painless or mildly uncomfortable.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies peripheral neuropathy and arterial disease that change risk assessment for ulceration and surgical planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Pedal pulse palpation, 10-g monofilament sensory testing at 10 plantar sites, and vibration testing with a 128-Hz tuning fork. Painless and takes 5-10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Ankle-brachial index and Doppler if vascular disease suspected
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies peripheral arterial disease before considering surgery; essential in diabetic and elderly patients
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood pressure cuffs applied at the arms and ankles and Doppler used to measure pressures. Total 20-30 minutes. ABI under 0.9 suggests arterial disease and warrants vascular consultation.

treatmentOverview: Initial management of every hammer toe is conservative. Footwear modification is the single most effective intervention: shoes with a wide and deep toe box, soft uppers, and low heels (under 2.5 cm) reduce pressure on the bent joint and slow progression. Toe pads (silicone hammer-toe cushions, crest pads) redistribute pressure away from the corn; metatarsal pads relieve plantar callus pain. Strapping the affected toe to its neighbor (buddy taping) and night splints maintain a corrected position in flexible deformities. Stretching the long flexor tendon with daily towel scrunches and intrinsic foot strengthening (short-foot exercises) help in mild deformity. Corns are debrided gently with an emery board or by a podiatrist; sharp blade debridement is reserved for clinic settings. Custom orthoses correct underlying biomechanical drivers (low arch, equinus). Conservative care controls symptoms in roughly 60-70% of flexible deformities long term. For pain that limits function despite optimal conservative care or for rigid deformities, surgery is offered. The most common procedures are PIP joint arthroplasty (removing the head of the proximal phalanx, leaving a stable shortened toe), PIP joint arthrodesis (fusing the joint in a straight position with a Kirschner wire or intramedullary implant), tenotomy of the flexor digitorum longus, and flexor-to-extensor tendon transfer for plantar plate insufficiency. Concurrent bunion or metatarsal procedures are common. Success rates exceed 80% for symptom relief and 70% for full cosmetic correction; complications include floating toe, recurrence, malalignment, transfer metatarsalgia, and nerve injury. Patients with diabetes or vascular disease are managed cautiously because of ulceration and wound-healing concerns; in selected cases, prophylactic correction reduces ulceration risk in high-risk feet.

medicalTreatments.name: Footwear modification (wide, deep toe box, low heel)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line intervention for all hammer toes. Switching to shoes with at least 1.5 cm of depth over the toes, soft uppers, and heels under 2.5 cm dramatically reduces pressure-related pain.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom control in 60-70% of flexible deformities with consistent use

medicalTreatments.name: Hammer-toe pads, crest pads, and silicone splints
medicalTreatments.description: Off-the-shelf cushions worn over the bent joint or under the toes redistribute pressure and protect the corn. Daily use is well tolerated and significantly reduces shoe-related pain.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction in approximately 60-70% of patients in observational cohorts

medicalTreatments.name: Metatarsal pads and orthotic insoles
medicalTreatments.description: Offload the metatarsal heads and address underlying biomechanical drivers. Over-the-counter or custom-made; combined with footwear adjustment they provide longest-lasting non-surgical control.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Plantar callus pain reduction in 60-70%; modest improvement in walking comfort

medicalTreatments.name: Buddy taping and night splints
medicalTreatments.description: Strapping the affected toe to the neighboring toe in the day or wearing a night splint maintains a more neutral position in flexible deformities and may slow progression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for symptom control and slowing progression in flexible deformities; less useful in rigid cases

medicalTreatments.name: Corn debridement (clinic-based)
medicalTreatments.description: Sharp paring of hyperkeratotic skin over the bent joint by a podiatrist. Repeated every 6-12 weeks for symptomatic relief. Should not be performed at home in diabetic or vascular-compromised patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Immediate pain relief and reduced shoe friction lasting 6-12 weeks per treatment

medicalTreatments.name: Toe-strengthening exercises (short-foot, towel scrunch)
medicalTreatments.description: Daily intrinsic foot muscle exercises strengthen the lumbricals and interossei that counterbalance the extensor and long flexor tendons. Most effective in flexible deformities and as adjunct to other care.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom and functional improvement in 50-60% of flexible deformities over 8-12 weeks

surgicalOptions.name: PIP joint arthroplasty (Post-Schwartzberg head resection)
surgicalOptions.description: Resection of the head of the proximal phalanx and capsular release at the PIP joint, with temporary Kirschner-wire fixation. Most common procedure for rigid hammer toe. Outpatient day surgery under local or regional anesthesia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Patient satisfaction 80-90%; full cosmetic correction in approximately 70%; recurrence 10-15% at 5 years

surgicalOptions.name: PIP joint arthrodesis (fusion with K-wire or intramedullary implant)
surgicalOptions.description: Permanent fusion of the PIP joint in a straight position. Preferred over arthroplasty when greater stability is needed (e.g. plantar plate insufficiency, neuromuscular disease). Various implants available including absorbable and metal devices.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Bony union in 80-90%; symptomatic relief in 85-95%; floating toe in 5-10%

surgicalOptions.name: Flexor digitorum longus tenotomy
surgicalOptions.description: Percutaneous release of the long flexor tendon under local anesthesia. Used in flexible deformities, particularly in older patients and in those with plantar callus and impending ulceration in diabetes.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effective symptom relief in 70-80% of flexible deformities; minimal scarring and rapid recovery

surgicalOptions.name: Flexor-to-extensor tendon transfer (Girdlestone-Taylor)
surgicalOptions.description: The long flexor tendon is divided and rerouted to the extensor hood, restoring intrinsic-like balance. Used in flexible deformities with plantar plate dysfunction or in combination with PIP procedures.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cosmetic and functional satisfaction in 75-85%; effective for plantar plate-driven deformity

surgicalOptions.name: Plantar plate repair
surgicalOptions.description: Open or arthroscopic-assisted repair of a torn plantar plate at the lesser MTP joint, often combined with PIP procedure. Restores stability at the MTP joint and prevents recurrence of hammer toe.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Subjective improvement in 75-85%; reduction of MTP instability in 80-90%

alternativeTreatments.name: Manual therapy and joint mobilization
alternativeTreatments.description: Physiotherapist or podiatrist-led mobilization of the MTP and PIP joints may improve range of motion in semirigid deformities. Best used as adjunct to footwear and exercises rather than standalone therapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Toe yoga and short-foot exercise progressions
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured intrinsic foot muscle training programs (toe yoga, short-foot exercise) build strength in the lumbricals and interossei. Evidence is limited but the programs are low-risk and complement other measures.

whySeeSpecialist: Podiatric referral is appropriate for any painful or progressive hammer toe, recurrent corns, plantar callus, or ulceration risk. Foot and ankle orthopedic surgery is involved when surgical correction is being considered, particularly in combination with bunion or plantar plate procedures. Patients with diabetes or vascular disease benefit from coordinated care including vascular surgery and endocrinology.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a podiatrist or foot and ankle surgeon experienced in lesser toe deformity correction and high-risk diabetic foot care. Centers that offer same-day imaging, biomechanical assessment, custom orthotics, and high-volume forefoot surgery deliver the best outcomes. Verify experience with both arthroplasty and arthrodesis techniques.
hospitalCriteria: Podiatric clinic with weight-bearing radiograph capability
Custom orthotic fabrication on site or through close network referral
Outpatient surgery facility with regional anesthesia
Multidisciplinary diabetic foot service for high-risk patients
Vascular surgery and endocrinology backup for at-risk patients
keyFacilities: Foot and ankle orthopedic surgery centers
preventionStrategies: Wear shoes with a toe box at least one finger-width longer than the longest toe and at least 1.5 cm deep, with soft uppers.
Keep heels under 2.5 cm for daily wear; reserve higher heels for occasional short use.
Replace worn shoes every 6-12 months; deformed footwear loses its supportive shape.
Stretch the toe flexors and strengthen the intrinsic foot muscles daily (towel scrunches, marble pickups, short-foot exercise).
Maintain a healthy body weight; excess body mass increases forefoot pressure.
Treat coexisting bunion, plantar plate dysfunction, and equinus early to prevent secondary lesser-toe deformity.
lifestyleModifications: Inspect the feet daily for corns, calluses, or skin breakdown, especially in diabetes.
Use moisturizer to soften skin around corns; avoid medicated corn plasters in vascular or diabetic feet because of salicylic-acid skin damage risk.
Replace footwear before significant deformation of the upper occurs.
Use silicone toe sleeves and crest pads during long walking days.
Engage in low-impact exercise (swimming, cycling) when walking is painful.
Attend regular podiatric reviews — annually for most adults, every 3-6 months in diabetes or established deformity.

recommended: Mediterranean-style anti-inflammatory diet supporting joint and connective tissue health
Adequate protein intake (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) for healthy soft-tissue maintenance
Vitamin D and calcium-rich foods for bone health, especially in postmenopausal women
Adequate hydration to support skin integrity
avoid: Excessive alcohol — interferes with bone and tendon healing after surgery
Tobacco use — major risk factor for poor wound healing and surgical site infection
Excessive added sugar in diabetes — directly affects neuropathy and ulceration risk
Crash diets that produce rapid weight loss and bone loss

exerciseGuidelines: Low-impact aerobic activities (swimming, cycling, elliptical training) are well tolerated. Walking is encouraged in supportive footwear. Daily intrinsic foot strengthening exercises (towel scrunches, marble pickups, short-foot exercise) and calf-stretching reduce extensor overpull on the lesser toes. After surgery, weight-bearing in a postoperative shoe begins immediately for most procedures; full sport returns at 8-12 weeks.
dailyManagement: Wear roomy, supportive footwear throughout the day.
Apply hammer-toe pads or silicone cushions before activities that have previously caused pain.
Inspect the feet daily for skin breakdown or new corns.
Perform stretching and strengthening exercises daily for 5-10 minutes.
Trim toenails straight across and file corns gently with an emery board; avoid sharp blades at home.
Schedule podiatric reviews at recommended intervals.
prognosis: Mild flexible hammer toes respond well to footwear changes, padding, and exercises, with most patients reporting good symptom control over years. Untreated deformity tends to progress slowly from flexible to rigid over 5-15 years. Surgical correction delivers patient satisfaction in 80-90%, full cosmetic correction in 70-80%, and durable symptom relief in over 85% at 5 years; recurrence is 10-15% and depends on whether underlying biomechanical drivers (bunion, plantar plate, equinus) have been addressed. Complications include floating toe (the toe sits dorsally above the floor), nonunion at fusion sites, transfer metatarsalgia, neuroma, and persistent stiffness. Major adverse outcomes are rare in non-diabetic patients with normal vasculature. In diabetes or peripheral vascular disease, the principal risks are ulceration over the bent joint, infection, and amputation — which justifies more aggressive prophylactic correction in selected high-risk feet.
recoveryTimeline: Conservative management: symptomatic improvement within 2-4 weeks of footwear change and padding. Postoperative recovery depends on procedure: percutaneous tenotomy, return to normal shoes in 1-2 weeks; PIP arthroplasty with K-wire, wire removal at 4-6 weeks, full activity at 6-8 weeks; PIP arthrodesis with implant, bony union at 6-12 weeks, full athletic activity at 10-16 weeks.
complications: Recurrent or persistent deformity in 10-15% after surgery, often related to under-corrected coexisting bunion or plantar plate dysfunction.
Floating toe (the operated toe sits dorsally above floor level) in 5-10% after PIP fusion, particularly when plantar plate is not addressed.
Transfer metatarsalgia — new pain under an adjacent metatarsal head from altered forefoot mechanics.
Ulceration and osteomyelitis over the bent joint or under the metatarsal head in patients with neuropathy or vascular disease.
Postoperative infection, malunion, nonunion, neuroma, and chronic regional pain syndrome in rare cases.
Vascular compromise of the toe after surgery — uncommon but serious; managed by immediate release of constricting dressings and vascular assessment.

supportResources.name: American Podiatric Medical Association (APMA)
supportResources.description: US podiatric professional society; patient education on hammer toe, find-a-podiatrist tool, and footwear approval program.

supportResources.name: American Orthopaedic Foot & Ankle Society (AOFAS)
supportResources.description: Patient-facing condition pages on hammer toe, claw toe, and bunion; find-a-surgeon directory.

supportResources.name: NHS — Hammer toe
supportResources.description: Concise public-facing guidance on diagnosis, conservative care, and when to consider surgery.

supportResources.name: The College of Podiatry (UK)
supportResources.description: UK podiatric professional body with patient-facing condition information and practitioner directory.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bunion (Hallux Valgus)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bunion is a deformity of the great toe at the MTP joint with lateral deviation of the hallux. Hammer toe is a bent lesser toe at the PIP joint. The two often coexist — the bunion crowds the second toe and accelerates hammer-toe formation. Treatment is usually combined.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hallux Rigidus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hallux rigidus is degenerative stiffness of the great toe MTP joint causing pain on push-off. Hammer toe affects the smaller toes at the PIP joint. The two have different causes, mechanisms, and treatments though both produce shoe-related pain.

confusedWithConditions.name: Plantar Fasciitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Plantar fasciitis causes inferior heel pain, especially with the first steps in the morning, from inflammation of the plantar fascia at the calcaneus. Hammer toe causes pain over the bent PIP joint and under the metatarsal heads. They can coexist but are anatomically and mechanically distinct.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hallux Valgus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hallux valgus is the medical term for bunion, with lateral deviation of the great toe and prominence of the first metatarsal head. Hammer toe affects the lesser toes; the two deformities frequently coexist and are often corrected together in the same surgical setting.

relatedConditions.name: Bunion (Hallux Valgus)
relatedConditions.relevance: Most common coexisting forefoot deformity; combined correction yields better outcomes than addressing either alone.

relatedConditions.name: Plantar Fasciitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequent companion of hammer toe in patients with biomechanical foot dysfunction and altered weight-bearing patterns.

relatedConditions.name: Hallux Rigidus
relatedConditions.relevance: Another degenerative forefoot deformity; both lead to shoe-related pain and altered gait.

relatedConditions.name: Achilles Tendinitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Calf tightness drives forefoot overload that contributes to hammer toe; stretching the gastrocnemius is part of conservative management.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Inflammatory arthritis classically produces combined forefoot deformities including hammer toes, claw toes, and bunions ('rheumatoid forefoot').

faqs.question: What is a hammer toe?
faqs.answer: Hammer toe is a deformity in which one of the smaller toes is permanently bent downward at its middle joint, often with the toe tip pointing toward the ground. The bent joint rubs against the top of the shoe and produces painful corns. The second toe is most commonly affected.

faqs.question: What causes a hammer toe?
faqs.answer: The main causes are tight or high-heeled shoes worn over years, inherited foot shape (long second toe, high arches), bunions that crowd the lesser toes, neuromuscular disorders, foot trauma, and inflammatory arthritis. The deformity reflects an imbalance between long flexor, long extensor, and intrinsic foot muscles.

faqs.question: How do I know if my hammer toe is flexible or rigid?
faqs.answer: Push gently upward under the ball of the foot and see whether the toe straightens. Full straightening means a flexible deformity; partial means semirigid; no change means rigid. A podiatrist or foot surgeon performs this test as part of every hammer-toe assessment.

faqs.question: Can hammer toes be fixed without surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes, in most flexible deformities. Wide, deep shoes, hammer-toe pads, metatarsal cushions, buddy taping, night splints, toe-strengthening exercises, and periodic corn debridement control symptoms in 60-70% of patients. Conservative care is the first step before considering surgery.

faqs.question: When is surgery needed for hammer toes?
faqs.answer: Surgery is considered for rigid deformities that cannot be straightened, painful deformities not relieved by 3-6 months of conservative care, recurrent ulceration or skin breakdown over the bent joint, and patients whose footwear options are severely limited. Various procedures are available including arthroplasty, arthrodesis, and tendon transfer.

faqs.question: How is hammer toe surgery performed?
faqs.answer: Most procedures are outpatient day cases under local or regional anesthesia. A small incision over the bent joint allows resection of bone (arthroplasty) or fusion of the joint in a straight position (arthrodesis), often with a temporary Kirschner wire or internal implant. Patients walk in a postoperative shoe immediately and progress to normal shoes over 6-8 weeks.

faqs.question: How long does hammer toe surgery recovery take?
faqs.answer: Most patients wear a postoperative stiff-soled shoe for 4-6 weeks. Kirschner wires are removed at 4-6 weeks if used. Return to athletic shoes typically at 6-8 weeks, fashion shoes at 3-4 months, and full sport at 8-12 weeks. Swelling can persist for 3-6 months.

faqs.question: What shoes are best for hammer toes?
faqs.answer: Shoes with a wide and deep toe box (at least 1.5 cm of vertical room above the bent joint), soft uppers, a low heel under 2.5 cm, and supportive arches reduce pressure on the affected toe. Specific brands marketed for diabetic, comfort, or orthopedic use are often suitable. Avoid pointed, narrow, or high-heeled styles.

faqs.question: Are hammer toes painful?
faqs.answer: Many hammer toes are initially painless cosmetic deformities. Pain develops once corns form over the bent joint, calluses form under the metatarsal head, the toe becomes rigid, or skin breakdown occurs. Roughly half of patients with mild hammer toes have significant footwear-related pain.

faqs.question: Can I prevent hammer toes?
faqs.answer: Wearing shoes with a wide and deep toe box and low heels, maintaining strong intrinsic foot muscles through daily exercises, addressing bunions early, and controlling diabetes if present all reduce the risk of developing hammer toes. Genetic predisposition cannot be changed, but progression can usually be slowed substantially.

faqs.question: Do hammer toes get worse over time?
faqs.answer: Most hammer toes progress slowly from flexible to rigid over 5-15 years if footwear and biomechanical drivers are not addressed. Co-existing bunions, plantar plate dysfunction, neuromuscular disease, and inflammatory arthritis all accelerate progression. Early conservative care slows but does not always halt progression.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hammer toe, claw toe, and mallet toe?
faqs.answer: Hammer toe is flexion of the middle (PIP) joint with the tip pointing down. Claw toe is flexion of both middle and end joints with hyperextension of the metatarsophalangeal joint. Mallet toe is flexion of the end (DIP) joint alone. All three reflect muscle-tendon imbalance in the lesser toes.

faqs.question: Can children get hammer toes?
faqs.answer: Yes, but pediatric hammer toes are uncommon and usually reflect a neuromuscular condition (cerebral palsy, Charcot-Marie-Tooth) or congenital deformity rather than footwear. Children with persistent toe deformity should be evaluated by a pediatric podiatrist or foot and ankle surgeon to look for underlying causes.

faqs.question: Is hammer toe a sign of diabetes?
faqs.answer: Not directly, but diabetes is a recognized risk factor through motor neuropathy weakening the intrinsic foot muscles. Diabetic patients with hammer toes face higher ulceration and amputation risk over the bent joint, so early podiatric care, footwear modification, and consideration of prophylactic correction are important.

faqs.question: Do toe straighteners and splints work?
faqs.answer: Toe straighteners and silicone splints provide symptomatic relief in flexible deformities and slow progression in some patients but do not permanently correct an established hammer toe. They are most effective combined with footwear modification, intrinsic foot strengthening, and addressing coexisting bunion or plantar plate dysfunction.

faqs.question: What is a corn and how is it related to hammer toe?
faqs.answer: A corn is hyperkeratosis (thickened skin) that develops where bony prominences chronically rub against footwear. The dorsal surface of the bent PIP joint is the classic site in hammer toe. Corns are painful and indicate that footwear pressure is excessive; gentle debridement, padding, and shoe change resolve most corns.

faqs.question: Can I cut my own corn at home?
faqs.answer: Gentle filing with an emery board after softening the skin in warm water is reasonable for most people. Sharp blades and corn plasters containing salicylic acid should be avoided, especially in diabetes or vascular disease, because skin injury can lead to ulceration. A podiatrist can debride corns safely in the clinic.

faqs.question: Will my hammer toe come back after surgery?
faqs.answer: Recurrence occurs in 10-15% of cases over 5 years and is more common when coexisting deformities (bunion, plantar plate dysfunction, equinus) were not corrected at the time of surgery. Properly selected combined procedures and ongoing footwear care minimize recurrence.

faqs.question: Does insurance cover hammer toe surgery?
faqs.answer: Most insurance plans cover hammer toe surgery when conservative treatment has failed and the deformity causes documented functional impairment, pain, or ulceration. Purely cosmetic correction is not typically covered. Preauthorization and documentation by a podiatrist or foot surgeon are required.

faqs.question: Are there exercises that help hammer toes?
faqs.answer: Daily intrinsic foot exercises help: towel scrunches (gripping a towel with the toes), marble pickups, short-foot exercise (pulling the ball of the foot toward the heel while keeping the toes flat), and toe yoga. Combined with calf stretching and footwear modification, they slow progression and reduce symptoms in flexible deformities.

faqs.question: Can hammer toes cause back or knee pain?
faqs.answer: Yes, indirectly. Pain at the toes alters gait, which can overload the knees, hips, and lower back over time. Treating the toe deformity, restoring normal gait, and providing supportive footwear reduce these secondary symptoms in many patients.

metaTitle: Hammer Toe: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Hammer toe: bent lesser toe causing corns and shoe pain. Causes, exercises, splints, footwear modification and surgical correction options, reviewed.
